attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-year history of intensely itchy dark papules on his anterior lower legs and forearms. He reports frequent scratching of the areas. He has impaired glucose intolerance and takes no regular medications. He works as a chef.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple hyperpigmented, slightly raised macules and papules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms and anterior lower limbs. The lesions have a rippled appearance. There is no scaling or excoriation. A punch biopsy demonstrates amyloid deposits and apple-green birefringence on congo-red stain.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of lichen amyloidosis, a form of primary localised cutaneous amyloidosis. This is the most common type of cutaneous amyloidosis, and tends to occur in patients in their 50s and of Asia, South American or Indian background. It is rare to present in Caucasians. The characteristic findings include pruritic red to brown macules and papules, typically occurring on the lower limbs and less frequently on the upper limbs, back and head. The condition may be triggered by repeated scratching and friction however the cause of the amyloid deposits is unclear. The diagnosis is confirmed by the presence of amyloid deposits on skin biopsy and congo-red staining.

Unfortunately, management is more supportive and symptoms commonly reoccur. There may be crossover with atopic dermatitis and macular amyloidosis in some cases.

Topical corticosteroids are the first-line treatment for symptomatic relief in localised cutaneous amyloidosis. They help reduce pruritus and inflammation, which may help break the itch-scratch cycle that contributes to disease progression.

Oral antihistamines may provide some symptomatic relief of pruritus but are not as effective as topical corticosteroids for this condition. They can be used as an adjunct therapy but are not considered first-line treatment.

Phototherapy with narrowband UVB or PUVA can be effective for extensive or refractory cases but is not appropriate as first-line therapy given its cost, inconvenience, and potential side effects.

Systemic retinoids such as acitretin are reserved for severe or widespread disease that has failed other treatments. They carry significant side effects and require careful monitoring. It is not appropriate initial management of cutaneous amyloidosis.

Topical calcineurin inhibitors like tacrolimus have shown some benefit in case reports but are not considered first-line therapy due to limited evidence and higher cost compared to topical corticosteroids. Other systemic treatments such as human anti-IL-4 receptor-α monoclonal antibody may be considered with specialist input for resistance disease.
learningPoints: Primary cutaneous amyloidosis is a chronic condition characterised by pruritic hyperpigmented macules and papules.
Topical corticosteroids are first-line treatment for symptomatic relief in localised cutaneous amyloidosis.
references: Ngan V, Aung T, Coulson I. Cutaneous amyloidosis. Dermnet NZ. 2022. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/cutaneous-amyloidosis

Bandhlish A, Aggarwal A, Koranne RV. A clinico-epidemiological study of macular amyloidosis from north India. Indian J Dermatol. 2012 Jul;57(4):269-74. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3401840/

Zhu Q, Gao BQ, Zhang JF, Shi LP, Zhang GQ. Successful treatment of lichen amyloidosis coexisting with atopic dermatitis by dupilumab: Four case reports. World J Clin Cases. 2023 Apr 16;11(11):2549-2558.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10131001/
